title: R Intro
content:
R Introduction

Statistical Powerhouse

R is a powerful programming language and environment specifically designed for statistical computing, data analysis, and graphical representation. It is widely used by statisticians, data scientists, and researchers for everything from simple data manipulation to complex machine learning models.

title: Hello World
content:
Hello World in R

Start Coding

R makes it incredibly easy to output text. You can use the print() function or simply type the string directly in the console.

script.R
# This is a comment in R
print("Hello, World!")

# Or just the string
"Hello, R!"

title: R Syntax
content:
R Basic Syntax

The Assignment Operator

Unlike many other languages that use = for assignment, R traditionally uses the <- operator. While = works in most cases, <- is the standard for R developers.

R Syntax Rules
# Assigning a value to a variable
name <- "Sanjith"
version <- 4.2

# Statements do not require semicolons
print(name)
print(version)

title: Data Types
content:
Variables & Data Types

Dynamic Typing

R is dynamically typed. The most basic data types in R are: numeric (floats/integers), integer (whole numbers with L suffix), complex (imaginary numbers), logical (TRUE/FALSE), and character (text).

Variable Types
num <- 10.5        # Numeric
int <- 10L         # Integer
logi <- TRUE       # Logical
char <- "R Stats"  # Character

# Check the class of a variable
print(class(num))
print(class(logi))

title: Operators
content:
R Operators

Calculations

R supports standard math and logical operators. Note that R uses & for AND and | for OR.

  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, ^ (Exponent), %% (Modulo)
  • Logical: & (AND), | (OR), ! (NOT)
  • Comparison: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=

title: If/Else
content:
If/Else Statements

Logical Decisions

Decisions are made using the if and else structure, similar to Java or C++.

Conditional Logic
x <- 10

if (x > 15) {
  print("Larger than 15")
} else if (x == 10) {
  print("It is exactly 10")
} else {
  print("Smaller than 10")
}

title: Loops
content:
R Loops

Iteration

Loops in R are useful when you need to repeat a task. The two most common are for and while loops.

For & While Loops
# For Loop: Iterating over a sequence
for (i in 1:3) {
  print(paste("Loop iteration:", i))
}

# While Loop: Continues while condition is true
j <- 1
while (j <= 3) {
  print(paste("While counter:", j))
  j <- j + 1
}

title: Vectors
content:
R Vectors

The Fundamental Structure

A vector is the simplest data structure in R. It contains items of the same data type. You combine items into a vector using the c() function.

Vector Creation
# Create a vector of numbers
nums <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)

# Create a vector of strings
fruits <- c("Apple", "Banana", "Cherry")

# Accessing elements (R uses 1-based indexing!)
print(fruits[1]) # Outputs Apple

title: Lists
content:
R Lists

Mixed Types

Unlike vectors, lists can contain elements of different data types, including numbers, strings, vectors, and even other lists.

List Example
my_list <- list("Apple", 10, TRUE, c(1, 2, 3))

# Printing list contents
print(my_list)

# Accessing elements via double brackets
print(my_list[[1]])

title: Matrices
content:
R Matrices

2D Data

A matrix is a two-dimensional data structure where all elements have the same data type. It has rows and columns.

Matrix Creation
# Create a 2x3 matrix
my_matrix <- matrix(c(1:6), nrow = 2, ncol = 3)

print(my_matrix)

# Access row 1, col 2
print(my_matrix[1, 2])

title: Data Frames
content:
R Data Frames

Tabular Data

Data frames represent tables of data. Each column can contain a different data type. This is the most common format for reading data files in R.

Creating Data Frames
df <- data.frame(
  Name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"),
  Age = c(25, 30, 22),
  Passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

print(df)
summary(df) # Get statistical info

title: Functions
content:
R Functions

Reusable Code

A function is a block of code which only runs when it is called. You can create your own functions using the function() keyword.

Custom Functions
# Defining a greeting function
say_hi <- function(name) {
  print(paste("Hello,", name))
}

# Calling the function
say_hi("Sanjith")

title: Visualization
content:
Data Visualization

Built-in Plotting

R is world-famous for its plotting capabilities. The basic plot() function can create many types of charts automatically.

R Plotting
x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
y <- c(4, 7, 8, 9, 10)

# Create a scatter plot
plot(x, y, main="My First Plot", xlab="X-Axis", ylab="Y-Axis")

# Drawing a line over it
lines(x, y, col="blue")

title: R Factors
content:
R Factors (Categories)

Categorical Data

Factors are used to categorize data and store it as levels. They are essential for statistical modeling and dealing with data that has a limited number of unique values (like "Male"/"Female" or "True"/"False").

Factor Example
# Create a factor
gender <- factor(c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male"))

# Check the levels
print(levels(gender))

# Summary shows counts for each category
summary(gender)

title: Statistics
content:
R Statistics

Built-in Stats

R was built for statisticians, so it has many built-in functions to perform calculations on datasets easily.

Statistical Functions
data <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60)

print(mean(data))   # Average
print(median(data)) # Middle value
print(min(data))    # Minimum
print(max(data))    # Maximum
print(sd(data))     # Standard Deviation
print(range(data))    # Min and Max values

title: Reading Data
content:
Reading Data Files

Importing Datasets

In the real world, you'll rarely type data manually. R makes it easy to read files like CSV (Comma Separated Values) or Excel documents.

Data Import
# Read a CSV file into a Data Frame
my_data <- read.csv("dataset.csv")

# View the first few rows
head(my_data)

# Get the structure of the data
str(my_data)

title: R Packages
content:
R Packages (CRAN)

Extending R

R has a massive ecosystem of libraries (packages) available via CRAN. You can install thousands of specialized tools for any field of research.

Package Management
# Install a package (only need to do once)
# install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load a package (do every session)
library(ggplot2)

# Check installed packages
# library()

title: ggplot2 Intro
content:
Intro to ggplot2

Professional Graphics

ggplot2 is the most famous R package for data visualization. It uses the "Grammar of Graphics" to build layers of plots (data, mapping, and geometries).

ggplot2 Syntax
library(ggplot2)

# Creating a scatter plot with ggplot2
ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(title = "Weight vs MPG Plot")

title: R Summary
content:
R Basics Summary

Data Mastery Achieved

Congratulations! You have covered the fundamentals of R programming and are ready to dive into advanced data science. You've learned how to manipulate data, perform statistics, and create professional visualizations.

  • Core Concepts: Variables, Assignment (<-), and dynamic typing.
  • Data Structures: Vectors, Lists, Matrices, and Data Frames.
  • Advanced Tools: Factors for categorical data and R Packages for extended functionality.
  • Data Analysis: Reading external files, built-in stats, and advanced plotting with ggplot2.

Continue practicing with real-world datasets from repositories like Kaggle to master the true power of R!
